title: Oncology Endocrinopathies: 20 Diagnostic Questions
subtitle: Treatment Attribution, ICI/TKI Profiles & Axis-by-Axis Evaluation in Cancer Patients
authors: User-provided clinical framework (chat); key ICI timing and thyroiditis claims cross-checked against Annual Review of Medicine (2020), Cleveland Clinic Journal of Medicine (2023), European Thyroid Journal best-practices (2025), and Endotext ICI endocrine irAE chapter

items.title: Why must you differentiate ICIs, TKIs, cytotoxic chemotherapy, and hormone-suppressive therapies?
items.content:
Each oncological drug class carries a distinct endocrine side-effect profile.
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) most commonly cause autoimmune endocrinopathies — thyroiditis, hypophysitis, adrenalitis, and insulin-dependent diabetes.
Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) such as sunitinib and sorafenib frequently cause primary hypothyroidism through a different mechanism.
Traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy and hormone-suppressive regimens (ADT, aromatase inhibitors) create yet another pattern dominated by gonadal suppression and bone loss.
Pinpointing the exact cause of an endocrine disorder in an oncology patient requires knowing which class is on board before ordering axis-specific workup.
keyPoints: ICIs → autoimmune endocrinopathies (thyroid, pituitary, adrenal, pancreas).
TKIs → frequent primary hypothyroidism (compare to baseline).
Cytotoxics and hormone-suppressive therapy → gonadal/bone effects dominate.
Drug class determines which of the 20 diagnostic questions matter most.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "You must differentiate between immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy, or hormone-suppressive therapies, as each has distinct endocrine side-effect profiles."

items.title: How does the chronological timeline distinguish treatment-induced endocrinopathy?
items.content:
Establish when cancer therapy started and when endocrine symptoms first appeared.
ICI-induced endocrinopathies classically emerge in the 6–12 week window after initiation — hypophysitis with CTLA-4 inhibitors (e.g., ipilimumab) most often at 8–10 weeks — but onset can occur months after treatment has stopped.
PD-1/PD-L1–associated hypophysitis tends to present later (median ~24–40 weeks in some cohorts), so a "normal" first three months does not exclude immune-mediated pituitary disease.
A symptom that predates all oncologic therapy is unlikely to be treatment-induced unless it represents unmasked pre-existing disease.
keyPoints: Map symptom onset to therapy start/stop dates.
ICI endocrinopathies often appear 6–12 weeks in (especially CTLA-4 hypophysitis ~8–10 weeks).
Late and post-treatment onset is well documented.
PD-1/PD-L1 hypophysitis may present substantially later than CTLA-4.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "When did the cancer therapy initiate, and when did the endocrine symptoms first appear? For example, ICI-induced endocrinopathies typically emerge 6 to 12 weeks post-initiation, but can occur months after treatment has stopped."

items.title: Why can high-dose corticosteroid taper mimic a new endocrine disorder?
items.content:
Dexamethasone and other glucocorticoids used for chemotherapy premedication, brain metastases, or antiemesis suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis.
When steroids are tapered or stopped, the patient may develop fatigue, nausea, hypotension, and hyponatremia that look identical to ICI-induced adrenal insufficiency.
The key question is whether symptoms track steroid withdrawal rather than a new immune event.
Always reconcile the steroid timeline before attributing adrenal dysfunction solely to checkpoint inhibition.
keyPoints: High-dose steroids suppress the HPA axis.
Taper/withdrawal can cause secondary adrenal suppression.
Symptoms overlap with ICI-induced hypophysitis/adrenal insufficiency.
Steroid timeline must be mapped before blaming ICIs alone.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "If the patient received steroids (e.g., dexamethasone for chemotherapy premedication or brain metastases) that are now being tapered, are their symptoms actually due to secondary adrenal suppression or steroid withdrawal?"

items.title: How does radiation field determine endocrine risk?
items.content:
Localized radiation can directly injure endocrine organs depending on the field.
Brain/skull-base radiation risks hypopituitarism from pituitary or hypothalamic damage.
Head-and-neck radiation risks primary hypothyroidism and hypoparathyroidism.
Mediastinal radiation can affect thyroid tissue in the field.
Radiation-induced endocrine failure may present months to years after treatment — a delayed timeline that must be distinguished from acute ICI toxicity.
keyPoints: Brain/skull base → hypopituitarism risk.
Head and neck → hypothyroidism, hypoparathyroidism.
Mediastinum → thyroid in field.
Radiation effects may be delayed months to years.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "Determine if radiation fields included the brain/skull base (risk for hypopituitarism), the head and neck (risk for primary hypothyroidism or hypoparathyroidism), or the mediastinum."

items.title: Why is ICI-induced hypophysitis a life-threatening emergency?
items.content:
Hypophysitis — pituitary inflammation — is most strongly associated with CTLA-4 inhibitors such as ipilimumab, though it can occur with PD-1/PD-L1 agents.
Presenting features include severe or atypical headache, profound unexplained lethargy, nausea, dizziness, and hyponatremia.
At diagnosis, most patients have central adrenal insufficiency; central hypothyroidism and hypogonadism are also common.
Because ACTH deficiency can precipitate adrenal crisis, hypophysitis must be recognized and treated urgently — not dismissed as cancer-related fatigue.
keyPoints: Most common with CTLA-4 inhibitors (ipilimumab); also reported with PD-1/PD-L1.
Headache, lethargy, nausea, hyponatremia are key clues.
Central adrenal insufficiency present in most cases at diagnosis.
Adrenal crisis risk makes this an emergency, not a watch-and-wait finding.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Is the patient experiencing severe, atypical headaches, visual field deficits, or profound, unexplained lethargy that would suggest pituitary inflammation (most common with CTLA-4 inhibitors like ipilimumab)?"

items.title: How do you recognize impending adrenal crisis in an oncology patient?
items.content:
Adrenal crisis presents with unexplained orthostatic hypotension, refractory nausea and vomiting, extreme weakness, severe abdominal pain, confusion, and electrolyte abnormalities.
In the ICI setting, crisis may arise from primary adrenalitis (adrenal destruction) or secondary insufficiency from hypophysitis.
Any hemodynamically unstable oncology patient with nonspecific GI symptoms deserves an urgent 8:00 AM cortisol and ACTH before attributing symptoms to chemotherapy side effects or sepsis alone.
keyPoints: Orthostatic hypotension, refractory nausea/vomiting, weakness, abdominal pain.
Can arise from primary adrenalitis or secondary hypophysitis.
Do not attribute hemodynamic instability to chemo toxicity without adrenal labs.
Check morning cortisol and ACTH urgently.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Is the patient exhibiting unexplained orthostatic hypotension, refractory nausea and vomiting, extreme weakness, or severe abdominal pain?"

items.title: Why must cortisol and ACTH be interpreted together?
items.content:
Primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis) shows low cortisol with inappropriately high ACTH as the pituitary attempts compensation.
Secondary adrenal insufficiency (hypophysitis) shows low cortisol with inappropriately low or normal ACTH because the pituitary cannot mount a response.
This distinction directs imaging (pituitary MRI for hypophysitis), predicts coexisting central hypothyroidism/hypogonadism, and determines whether mineralocorticoid replacement is needed (primary but not isolated secondary disease).
keyPoints: Primary AI: low cortisol + high ACTH.
Secondary AI (hypophysitis): low cortisol + low/normal ACTH.
Pattern guides pituitary imaging and hormone replacement strategy.
Mineralocorticoid replacement needed in primary, not typical secondary AI.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Have you checked these to distinguish between primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis, indicated by low cortisol and high ACTH) and secondary adrenal insufficiency (hypophysitis, indicated by low cortisol and inappropriately low/normal ACTH)?"

items.title: Why must adrenal insufficiency be ruled out or treated before starting thyroid hormone?
items.content:
Central hypothyroidism from hypophysitis often coexists with secondary adrenal insufficiency.
Levothyroxine increases metabolic demand; if cortisol is deficient, thyroid replacement can unmask or precipitate cardiovascular collapse and fatal adrenal crisis.
The rule is straightforward: treat or empirically cover adrenal insufficiency before initiating thyroid hormone replacement when central hypothyroidism is suspected in an ICI-treated patient.
keyPoints: Central hypothyroidism and secondary AI frequently coexist in hypophysitis.
Thyroid hormone raises metabolic demand and cortisol requirement.
Untreated AI + levothyroxine can precipitate fatal adrenal crisis.
Cover or treat adrenal insufficiency before starting thyroid replacement.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "If central hypothyroidism is suspected, have you ensured that adrenal insufficiency was ruled out or treated first? Initiating thyroid hormone replacement in a patient with untreated adrenal insufficiency can precipitate a fatal adrenal crisis."

items.title: How does a biphasic thyroid response signal ICI-induced destructive thyroiditis?
items.content:
The hallmark of ICI-related thyroid dysfunction is destructive thyroiditis: stored hormone release causes a brief thyrotoxic phase (palpitations, anxiety, weight loss — often mild or asymptomatic), followed by crash into hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain).
Many patients ultimately require long-term levothyroxine because the hypothyroid phase is often permanent.
This pattern differs from TKI-associated hypothyroidism, which typically presents as gradual primary hypothyroidism without a preceding hyperthyroid phase.
keyPoints: Destructive thyroiditis: transient thyrotoxicosis → hypothyroidism.
Thyrotoxic phase may be brief or asymptomatic.
Hypothyroid phase often permanent; levothyroxine commonly needed.
Distinct from gradual TKI-induced primary hypothyroidism.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "Did the patient experience brief symptoms of hyperthyroidism (palpitations, anxiety, weight loss) followed by a crash into hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain)? This destructive thyroiditis pattern is the hallmark of ICI-induced thyroid dysfunction."

items.title: Why compare current thyroid labs to pre-treatment baseline on TKIs?
items.content:
TKIs such as sunitinib and sorafenib frequently cause primary hypothyroidism through mechanisms distinct from ICI autoimmune thyroiditis.
A rising TSH with falling free T4 compared to the patient's own pre-treatment baseline strongly supports TKI effect rather than unrelated primary thyroid disease.
Baseline thyroid function should be documented before starting TKI therapy whenever possible so that on-treatment shifts are interpretable.
keyPoints: Sunitinib and sorafenib commonly cause hypothyroidism.
Compare on-treatment TSH/free T4 to pre-TKI baseline.
Rising TSH from personal baseline supports drug effect.
Mechanism differs from ICI destructive thyroiditis.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "If the patient is on a TKI (like sunitinib or sorafenib, which frequently cause hypothyroidism), how do their current TSH and Free T4 levels compare to their pre-treatment baseline?"

items.title: When do compressive neck symptoms redirect the differential away from pure drug toxicity?
items.content:
Goiter, neck tenderness, or dysphagia raise concern for direct tumor invasion of the thyroid or adjacent structures, radiation-induced fibrosis, or (rarely) painful subacute thyroiditis.
These localizing symptoms warrant neck imaging and may indicate structural disease rather than a purely systemic immune or TKI effect.
Absence of compressive symptoms supports a biochemical endocrinopathy workup as the primary pathway.
keyPoints: Goiter, neck tenderness, dysphagia → structural differential.
Consider tumor invasion, post-radiation change, painful thyroiditis.
Imaging may be required when localizing symptoms present.
Pure ICI thyroiditis is usually painless.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "Does the patient have a goiter, neck tenderness, or dysphagia that might indicate direct tumor invasion, radiation-induced changes, or (rarely) painful thyroiditis?"

items.title: Why is sudden polyuria and polydipsia a red flag on ICIs?
items.content:
Abrupt onset of excessive thirst and urination in a patient on immune checkpoint inhibitors should trigger immediate evaluation for new-onset insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus from autoimmune islet destruction.
Unlike gradual hyperglycemia in type 2 diabetes, ICI-associated diabetes can present explosively and may be the first sign of pancreatic autoimmunity.
Capillary glucose, venous glucose, and ketone assessment should not be delayed.
keyPoints: Sudden polyuria/polydipsia on ICIs → evaluate for new diabetes immediately.
Mechanism: autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells.
Presentation can be abrupt, unlike slow T2DM progression.
Check glucose and ketones without delay.
items.reference: IV. The Pancreatic & Metabolic Axes: "Is the patient suddenly excessively thirsty and urinating frequently, which are red flags for ICI-induced insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus?"

items.title: How does ICI-induced diabetes often present as diabetic ketoacidosis?
items.content:
ICI-related diabetes frequently resembles new-onset type 1 diabetes with absolute insulin deficiency.
Patients may present with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — high capillary glucose, ketonuria or ketonemia, acidosis — as the inaugural event rather than mild asymptomatic hyperglycemia.
Always check ketones when evaluating unexpected hyperglycemia in an ICI-treated patient; DKA requires urgent insulin and fluid resuscitation.
keyPoints: ICI diabetes can present as abrupt DKA.
Check capillary glucose and urine/blood ketones.
Resembles new-onset type 1–like insulin deficiency.
DKA requires urgent insulin and volume resuscitation.
items.reference: IV. The Pancreatic & Metabolic Axes: "What is the patient's current capillary blood glucose, and are there ketones present in the urine or blood? ICI-induced diabetes often presents abruptly as DKA."

items.title: Why distinguish de novo type 1–like diabetes from worsening type 2 on steroids or cancer stress?
items.content:
Known type 2 diabetes may worsen from glucocorticoids, infection, or metabolic stress without implying ICI-induced autoimmunity.
De novo insulin-dependent diabetes with low or undetectable C-peptide and positive autoantibodies in a previously nondiabetic patient strongly supports ICI-mediated islet destruction.
The distinction matters for lifelong insulin dependence, immunotherapy continuation decisions, and avoiding mislabeling steroid-induced hyperglycemia as a permanent autoimmune endocrinopathy.
keyPoints: T2DM can worsen from steroids, stress, infection — not necessarily ICI autoimmunity.
De novo insulin dependence in a previously nondiabetic patient is the red flag.
C-peptide and autoantibodies help separate mechanisms.
Guides long-term insulin need and irAE attribution.
items.reference: IV. The Pancreatic & Metabolic Axes: "Does the patient have a known history of Type 2 Diabetes that is simply poorly controlled due to cancer stress/steroids, or is this a completely new onset of pancreatic islet cell destruction?"

items.title: What systemic effects follow androgen deprivation or aromatase inhibitor therapy?
items.content:
Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) for prostate cancer and aromatase inhibitors for breast cancer intentionally suppress sex-steroid production.
Expected consequences include vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes), severe loss of libido, mood lability, and fatigue.
These are pharmacologic effects of the oncology regimen rather than unexpected immune toxicity — but they must still be distinguished from depression, disease progression, or unrelated endocrine disorders.
keyPoints: ADT and aromatase inhibitors intentionally suppress sex steroids.
Hot flashes, libido loss, mood lability are expected.
Distinguish pharmacologic effect from progression or unrelated disease.
Counsel and treat symptomatically when appropriate.
items.reference: V. Gonadal, Bone, and Parathyroid Health: "If the patient is on Androgen Deprivation Therapy (ADT) for prostate cancer or aromatase inhibitors for breast cancer, are they experiencing new-onset vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes), severe loss of libido, or mood lability?"

items.title: Why monitor bone mineral density during hormone-suppressive oncology regimens?
items.content:
ADT and aromatase inhibitors accelerate bone loss by lowering circulating androgens or estrogens.
Fragility fractures or a DEXA scan showing rapid decline in bone mineral density may be directly linked to the hormone-suppressive regimen rather than unrelated osteoporosis.
Baseline and serial bone density assessment, calcium/vitamin D optimization, and antiresorptive therapy should be considered per oncology/endocrine guidelines when risk is high.
keyPoints: Hormone-suppressive therapy accelerates bone loss.
Fragility fractures or falling DEXA T-scores may be treatment-related.
Not every fracture in a cancer patient is from metastatic disease.
Prophylactic bone protection may be indicated.
items.reference: V. Gonadal, Bone, and Parathyroid Health: "Has the patient experienced any fragility fractures, or does a recent DEXA scan show accelerated bone loss directly linked to their hormone-suppressive oncology regimen?"

items.title: How do abnormal calcium levels confound the endocrine picture in oncology?
items.content:
Hypocalcemia with muscle cramping or tetany after head-and-neck radiation suggests hypoparathyroidism.
Hypercalcemia with confusion or constipation may reflect hyperparathyroidism — but in oncology patients, malignancy-related hypercalcemia from bone metastases or PTHrP secretion is far more common and can mimic a primary parathyroid disorder.
Always interpret calcium in the context of malignancy burden, PTH level, and radiation history.
keyPoints: Post-neck radiation hypocalcemia → consider hypoparathyroidism.
Hypercalcemia in cancer → bone mets/PTHrP more likely than primary hyperparathyroidism.
Check PTH and malignancy context.
Do not assume every calcium abnormality is a primary endocrine disorder.
items.reference: V. Gonadal, Bone, and Parathyroid Health: "Are there signs of hypocalcemia (muscle cramping, tetany) suggesting hypoparathyroidism post-neck radiation, or hypercalcemia (confusion, constipation) that could indicate parathyroid issues or, more likely, bone metastases mimicking an endocrine disorder?"

items.title: Why does pre-existing autoimmunity increase suspicion for ICI endocrinopathy?
items.content:
Personal or family history of autoimmune disease — Hashimoto thyroiditis, vitiligo, Addison disease, type 1 diabetes — may predate oncology treatment.
ICIs can unmask or aggravate underlying autoimmunity rather than create an entirely de novo process.
A patient with pre-existing thyroid autoantibodies, for example, is at higher risk for ICI thyroid dysfunction; baseline autoantibody status helps interpret new biochemical abnormalities.
keyPoints: Pre-existing autoimmunity may be unmasked or worsened by ICIs.
Hashimoto, vitiligo, Addison, T1DM history matters.
Thyroid autoantibodies predict higher ICI thyroid irAE risk.
Baseline autoimmunity status aids attribution.
items.reference: VI. Confounding Factors & Baselines: "Is there a personal or family history of autoimmune diseases (e.g., Hashimoto's thyroiditis, vitiligo, Addison's disease) that pre-dated the oncological treatment and has simply been unmasked or aggravated?"

items.title: How can cancer progression mimic endocrine disease?
items.content:
Extreme fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss — symptoms common to many endocrinopathies — are also cardinal features of cancer cachexia, anemia, and tumor progression.
Before launching a full endocrine panel, assess whether the clinical picture tracks rising tumor burden, falling albumin, new metastases, or declining performance status.
Treatment-attributable endocrinopathy and disease progression can coexist; the framework requires parallel evaluation, not either-or thinking.
keyPoints: Fatigue, anorexia, weight loss occur in both endocrinopathy and progression.
Assess tumor burden, cachexia, anemia, performance status.
Do not assume every constitutional symptom is an irAE.
Progression and endocrinopathy can coexist.
items.reference: VI. Confounding Factors & Baselines: "Could the patient's current 'endocrine' symptoms—such as extreme fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss—actually be driven by cancer cachexia, anemia, or tumor progression?"

items.title: Which non-oncology medications commonly confound endocrine evaluation?
items.content:
Amiodarone can cause both hypothyroidism and thyrotoxicosis.
Opioids suppress the gonadal and adrenal axes and may lower testosterone and cortisol responses.
Glucocorticoids, as discussed, cause HPA suppression.
A complete medication reconciliation — including over-the-counter supplements — is mandatory before attributing laboratory abnormalities to cancer therapy alone.
keyPoints: Amiodarone → thyroid dysfunction.
Opioids → suppressed gonadal and adrenal axes.
Glucocorticoids → HPA axis suppression.
Full medication reconciliation required for attribution.
items.reference: VI. Confounding Factors & Baselines: "Is the patient taking other drugs known to alter endocrine function (e.g., amiodarone affecting the thyroid, or opioids suppressing the gonadal and adrenal axes) that are confounding the clinical picture?"

items.title: Why are visual field deficits a weaker clue for ICI hypophysitis than the source suggests?
items.content:
The original framework lists visual field deficits among hypophysitis clues.
Independent review of ICI hypophysitis cohorts finds visual field changes are rare — unlike classic sellar mass compression from other causes of hypophysitis.
ICI hypophysitis more often presents with headache, fatigue, nausea, and hyponatremia without chiasmal compression.
Clinicians should not withhold pituitary-axis testing because the visual fields are normal.
keyPoints: Source lists visual field deficits; literature reports them as rare in ICI hypophysitis.
Headache, fatigue, nausea, hyponatremia are more reliable clues.
Normal visual fields do not exclude hypophysitis.
Pituitary-axis labs remain indicated with compatible symptoms.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Is the patient experiencing severe, atypical headaches, visual field deficits, or profound, unexplained lethargy that would suggest pituitary inflammation (most common with CTLA-4 inhibitors like ipilimumab)?" — cross-checked: Cleveland Clinic Journal of Medicine (2023) notes ICI hypophysitis is "usually not accompanied by visual disturbances."

items.question: A 58-year-old man with metastatic melanoma started ipilimumab 9 weeks ago. He presents with severe headache, profound fatigue, and nausea. Morning labs: cortisol 2.1 µg/dL (low), ACTH 8 pg/mL (inappropriately low). TSH is also low. What is the most likely diagnosis and immediate priority?
options: Primary adrenal insufficiency from metastatic adrenal invasion; start fludrocortisone
ICI-induced hypophysitis with secondary adrenal insufficiency; glucocorticoid replacement before thyroid hormone
Euthyroid sick syndrome from cancer cachexia; observe
Steroid withdrawal from prior dexamethasone premedication; resume high-dose steroids indefinitely
items.explanation: Low cortisol with inappropriately low ACTH at ~9 weeks on ipilimumab is classic secondary adrenal insufficiency from hypophysitis. CTLA-4 hypophysitis typically presents around 8–10 weeks. Low TSH suggests coexisting central hypothyroidism. Glucocorticoids must be started before levothyroxine to avoid adrenal crisis. Primary AI would show high ACTH; cachexia does not explain the ACTH pattern; steroid withdrawal is possible but less likely without a recent taper history matching the timeline.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Have you checked these to distinguish between primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis, indicated by low cortisol and high ACTH) and secondary adrenal insufficiency (hypophysitis, indicated by low cortisol and inappropriately low/normal ACTH)?"

items.question: A woman on combined ipilimumab and nivolumab has confirmed central hypothyroidism (low free T4, low TSH). Morning cortisol is 3 µg/dL and ACTH is suppressed. She is hemodynamically stable. What is the correct next step before starting levothyroxine?
options: Start levothyroxine immediately because she is euthyroid on cortisol alone
Initiate physiologic glucocorticoid replacement, then start levothyroxine
Start levothyroxine and add hydrocortisone only if hypotension develops
Stop all ICIs permanently and observe without hormone replacement
items.explanation: Untreated adrenal insufficiency plus thyroid hormone replacement can precipitate fatal adrenal crisis. Glucocorticoid replacement must precede levothyroxine when central hypothyroidism and secondary AI coexist — a common hypophysitis pattern. Waiting for hypotension is unsafe; ICIs may often continue once hormones are replaced, but adrenal coverage cannot be deferred.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "Initiating thyroid hormone replacement in a patient with untreated adrenal insufficiency can precipitate a fatal adrenal crisis."

items.question: A 65-year-old with NSCLC on pembrolizumab had brief palpitations and anxiety at week 6; at week 10 she reports fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance. TSH is now elevated with low free T4. Thyroid uptake scan shows low uptake. What pattern does this represent?
options: Graves disease requiring methimazole
Destructive ICI thyroiditis with transient thyrotoxicosis followed by hypothyroidism
Central hypothyroidism from hypophysitis
Amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis type 1
items.explanation: Brief hyperthyroid symptoms followed by hypothyroidism with low uptake is the hallmark destructive thyroiditis pattern of ICI thyroid dysfunction. Graves would show sustained hyperthyroidism with high uptake. Central hypothyroidism shows low TSH, not elevated TSH. Amiodarone is not in the vignette.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "Did the patient experience brief symptoms of hyperthyroidism (palpitations, anxiety, weight loss) followed by a crash into hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain)? This destructive thyroiditis pattern is the hallmark of ICI-induced thyroid dysfunction."

items.question: A 52-year-old without prior diabetes started nivolumab 8 weeks ago. He presents with polyuria, polydipsia, glucose 486 mg/dL, and positive urine ketones. Which statement is most accurate?
options: This is expected steroid-induced hyperglycemia; hold ICIs and observe
This likely represents ICI-induced insulin-dependent diabetes and requires urgent insulin for possible DKA
Oral metformin alone is sufficient; ketones are a benign finding
This confirms pre-existing type 2 diabetes; no relation to immunotherapy
items.explanation: Sudden polyuria/polydipsia with hyperglycemia and ketones in a previously nondiabetic ICI patient is classic for autoimmune beta-cell destruction presenting as DKA. This requires urgent insulin and volume resuscitation, not observation or metformin monotherapy. Attribution to pre-existing T2DM is incorrect without prior diabetes history.
items.reference: IV. The Pancreatic & Metabolic Axes: "ICI-induced diabetes often presents abruptly as DKA."

items.question: Morning labs in an ICI-treated patient with hypotension and vomiting: cortisol 1.8 µg/dL, ACTH 112 pg/mL (elevated). What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Secondary adrenal insufficiency from hypophysitis
Primary adrenal insufficiency from ICI-related adrenalitis
Factitious Cushing syndrome
Normal stress response to chemotherapy
items.explanation: Low cortisol with elevated ACTH indicates primary adrenal insufficiency — the adrenal gland cannot produce cortisol and the pituitary compensates with high ACTH. Secondary hypophysitis shows low/normal ACTH. Factitious Cushing would suppress ACTH. This is pathologic, not a normal stress response.
items.reference: II. The Pituitary & Adrenal Axes (Life-Threatening Emergencies): "primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenalitis, indicated by low cortisol and high ACTH)"

items.question: A patient with brain metastases received dexamethasone 8 mg daily for 6 weeks, now tapered off over 2 weeks. Two weeks after the last dose she has fatigue, nausea, and BP 92/58 mmHg. ICI was never started. Best explanation?
options: ICI-induced hypophysitis
Secondary adrenal suppression from recent high-dose glucocorticoid taper
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Radiation-induced hypothyroidism
items.explanation: High-dose glucocorticoids suppress the HPA axis; symptoms after taper/withdrawal mimic adrenal insufficiency and must be distinguished from ICI toxicity. No ICI was given, making hypophysitis unlikely. The timeline tracks steroid withdrawal, not parathyroid or thyroid disease.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "If the patient received steroids (e.g., dexamethasone for chemotherapy premedication or brain metastases) that are now being tapered, are their symptoms actually due to secondary adrenal suppression or steroid withdrawal?"

items.question: A patient on sunitinib for RCC had pre-treatment TSH 1.8 mIU/L. After 3 months on drug, TSH is 9.4 mIU/L with low-normal free T4. She denies neck pain or goiter. Most likely cause?
options: ICI destructive thyroiditis
TKI-associated primary hypothyroidism compared to baseline
TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma
Subacute painful thyroiditis
items.explanation: Rising TSH from personal baseline on sunitinib supports TKI-induced primary hypothyroidism — a frequent TKI effect. No ICI is involved. TSH-secreting adenoma would cause high free T4. Painful thyroiditis is inconsistent with the painless presentation.
items.reference: III. The Thyroid Axis: "If the patient is on a TKI (like sunitinib or sorafenib, which frequently cause hypothyroidism), how do their current TSH and Free T4 levels compare to their pre-treatment baseline?"

items.question: A patient treated for laryngeal cancer with neck radiation 2 years ago presents with perioral numbness, carpopedal spasm, and calcium 7.1 mg/dL. PTH is low. Most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypercalcemia of malignancy from bone metastases
Radiation-induced hypoparathyroidism
Primary hyperparathyroidism
ICI-induced hypophysitis
items.explanation: Hypocalcemia with low PTH after head-and-neck radiation strongly suggests hypoparathyroidism from parathyroid gland injury. Hypercalcemia of malignancy and primary hyperparathyroidism present with high calcium. ICI hypophysitis is not suggested by isolated hypocalcemia with low PTH years after neck radiation.
items.reference: V. Gonadal, Bone, and Parathyroid Health: "Are there signs of hypocalcemia (muscle cramping, tetany) suggesting hypoparathyroidism post-neck radiation"

items.question: A patient on pembrolizumab has progressive fatigue and 8-kg weight loss over 6 weeks. CT shows new liver metastases. TSH and cortisol are normal. What is the most appropriate conclusion?
options: Normal TSH and cortisol exclude any clinical problem
Symptoms may primarily reflect disease progression/cachexia; endocrine labs do not exclude coexisting irAE
Start empiric levothyroxine for fatigue
Stop pembrolizumab permanently without further evaluation
items.explanation: Constitutional symptoms with new metastases may reflect progression and cachexia rather than endocrinopathy. Normal screening labs do not exclude all irAEs (e.g., evolving thyroiditis, intermittent AI). Empiric thyroid hormone without indication is unsafe; stopping ICI requires individualized assessment, not automatic reflex.
items.reference: VI. Confounding Factors & Baselines: "Could the patient's current 'endocrine' symptoms—such as extreme fatigue, anorexia, and weight loss—actually be driven by cancer cachexia, anemia, or tumor progression?"

items.question: When evaluating whether a new endocrine problem is treatment-related in an oncology patient, which initial step is most foundational?
options: Order a random prolactin only
Map the exact drug classes, treatment start/stop dates, and symptom onset timeline
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics for sepsis
Obtain DEXA scan before any laboratory testing
items.explanation: The framework begins with treatment history and timeline — differentiating ICIs, TKIs, cytotoxics, hormone suppression, steroids, and radiation fields — because each class predicts distinct endocrine risks and typical onset windows. Random single tests or imaging without timeline context cannot attribute causality.
items.reference: I. Treatment History & Timeline: "What exact oncological agents is the patient receiving?" and "What is the precise chronological timeline?"
